q: This code runs slow — nothing is printed yet. What's wrong?

opts.t: No action was called — transformations are lazy and never execute
withColumnRenamed is not a valid PySpark method
opts.why: withColumnRenamed is valid; the real issue is no action triggers execution.

opts.t: filter() must come after withColumnRenamed()
opts.why: Order doesn't matter here; the bug is that no action (show, write, count) was called.

why: PySpark transformations (filter, withColumnRenamed, join, select) are lazy — they build a DAG but read zero data. Execution only begins when an action like .show(), .count(), or .write() is called.
fs: "Add an action like .show() or .write() to trigger execution."

q: Driver crashes with OOM. What's the bug?

opts.t: Replace collect() with a UDF or mapPartitions so data stays on executors
Call .cache() before .collect() to speed it up
opts.why: cache() stores data on executors; collect() still pulls everything to the driver, causing the same OOM.

opts.t: Use .count() instead of .collect()
opts.why: count() returns a single long to the driver — it doesn't let you iterate rows.

why: .collect() pulls every row across the network into the driver's JVM heap. At 500 M rows the driver runs out of memory. Keep processing on executors with mapPartitions, foreach, or a UDF.
fs: "Keep the data on executors — never collect a huge DataFrame."

q: Which approach is faster for uppercasing a column?

opts.t: Option A — the UDF gives more control
opts.why: Python UDFs serialize each row to Python, process it, then deserialize back to JVM — row-by-row overhead at scale.

opts.t: Option B — F.upper() runs natively on the JVM
Both are identical; Spark optimizes UDFs automatically
opts.why: Spark cannot optimize inside a Python UDF black box; F.upper() is a Catalyst-aware native expression.

why: Python UDFs force Spark to serialize each row between the JVM and a Python worker (via pickle/Arrow), adding per-row overhead. Built-in F.upper() executes entirely on the JVM executor and participates in Catalyst optimizations.
fj: "Use the UDF — Python is more flexible."

q: This join OOMs. Pick the fix.

opts.t: events.join(broadcast(country_codes), 'country')
.cache() country_codes before the join
opts.why: Caching stores data on executors but doesn't remove the shuffle; the join still OOMs.

opts.t: events.repartition(1).join(country_codes, 'country')
opts.why: Funneling 500 M events into one partition guarantees the very OOM you're trying to avoid.

opts.t: Use a crossJoin instead
opts.why: A cross join produces the Cartesian product — massively more data, not less.

why: Wrapping the small side in broadcast() ships its 250 rows to every executor so the join happens locally (map-side join) — no shuffle of the large fact table and no driver bottleneck.
fs: "Broadcast the small side to skip the shuffle."

q: The pipeline re-reads S3 three times. Fix it.

opts.t: Call base.cache() before the three uses
Replace .count() with len(base.collect())
opts.why: collect() pulls all rows to the driver — worse, not better.

opts.t: Combine all three into one long method chain
opts.why: Chaining doesn't avoid re-reads; Spark still re-executes the source scan for each action.

why: Without cache(), each action re-executes the full lineage back to S3. Calling base.cache() (or persist()) after the first action stores the partitions in executor memory so subsequent actions skip the source scan.
fj: "Spark is smart enough to reuse the scan automatically."
fs: "cache() the DataFrame before the first action so later actions hit memory."

q: Which way to get per-user totals is faster?

opts.t: Option A — aggregation stays distributed on executors
Option B — Python dicts are faster than Spark overhead
opts.why: collect() first pulls every row to the driver heap (OOM risk), then iterates serially in Python — no parallelism at all.

opts.t: Both are equivalent for small datasets
opts.why: Even on small data, Option B still materializes all rows to the driver; groupBy().agg() is always the correct PySpark idiom.

why: groupBy().agg() runs a distributed partial-aggregation shuffle then merges results — data never leaves executors until a tiny summary arrives at the driver. collect() + Python loop is single-threaded and OOM-prone.
fs: "Always aggregate on executors; never collect to aggregate."

q: One task runs 100× longer than the rest. Fix it.

opts.t: Salt the key: add a random bucket suffix, aggregate, then re-aggregate after stripping the salt
Increase spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to 2000
opts.why: More partitions splits the non-skewed keys thinner but the hot 'unknown' partition still lands on one task.

opts.t: Call .repartition('country') before groupBy
opts.why: repartition by the skewed key concentrates 'unknown' rows into fewer partitions — makes skew worse.

opts.t: Filter out 'unknown' first, then groupBy
opts.why: Dropping the majority of rows corrupts the aggregation result.

why: A hot key causes one task to process 90% of the data (partition skew). Salting appends a random int (0–N) to the key so 'unknown_0'…'unknown_N' spread across N tasks; a second groupBy strips the suffix and merges partial sums.
fs: "Salt the hot key to spread it across multiple partitions, then re-aggregate."

q: Job scans 1 TB but only needs last-week rows. Fix it.

opts.t: Filter events before the join so less data is shuffled
Add .cache() after the join
opts.why: Caching stores the already-large joined output; it doesn't reduce the shuffle that built it.

opts.t: Use .select() to drop columns before the join
opts.why: Column pruning helps I/O but doesn't reduce the row count going into the shuffle.

why: Applying the date filter before the join (events.filter(...).join(users,'user_id')) slashes the rows going into the shuffle stage. Spark's Catalyst may push the predicate automatically, but making it explicit guarantees the smaller scan and smaller shuffle.
fs: "Push the filter before the join — smaller input means smaller shuffle."
